bibliographic_data.title: "It Became My Buddy, But I'm Not Afraid to Disagree": A Multi-Session Study of UX Evaluators Collaborating with Conversational AI Assistants
bibliographic_data.abstract: AI-assisted usability analysis can potentially reduce the time and effort of finding usability problems, yet little is known about how AI’s perceived expertise influences evaluators’ analytic strategies and perceptions over time. We ran a within-subjects, five-session study (six hours per participant) with 12 professional UX evaluators who worked with two conversational assistants designed to appear novice- or expert-like (differing in suggestion quantity and response accuracy). We logged behavioral measures (number of passes, suggestion acceptance rate), collected subjective ratings (trust, perceived efficiency), and conducted semi-structured interviews. Participants experienced an initial novelty effect and a subsequent dip in trust that recovered over time. Their efficiency improved as they shifted from a two-pass to a one-pass video inspection approach. Evaluators ultimately rated the experienced CA as significantly more efficient, trustworthy, and comprehensive, despite not perceiving expertise differences early on. We conclude with design implications for adapting AI expertise to enable calibrated human-AI collaboration.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study investigates how the perceived expertise of conversational AI assistants influences UX evaluators' analytic strategies and perceptions over multiple sessions, providing insights into human-AI collaboration in usability analysis.
core_content.key_methodology: A within-subjects, five-session study with 12 professional UX evaluators who worked with two types of conversational assistants (novice and experienced) designed to differ in suggestion quantity and response accuracy. Behavioral measures and subjective ratings were collected over time.
outcomes: Participants experienced an initial novelty effect followed by a dip in trust that recovered over time.
Evaluators' efficiency improved as they shifted from a two-pass to a one-pass video inspection approach.
The experienced CA was rated as significantly more efficient, trustworthy, and comprehensive.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This study explores the impact of conversational AI assistants on UX evaluators over multiple sessions. The research team conducted a within-subjects study with 12 professional UX evaluators who worked with two types of conversational assistants: novice and experienced. These assistants were designed to differ in their suggestion quantity and response accuracy. Behavioral measures such as the number of passes and suggestion acceptance rate, subjective ratings like trust and perceived efficiency, and semi-structured interviews were collected over five sessions totaling six hours per participant. The study found that participants initially experienced a novelty effect followed by a dip in trust which recovered over time. Evaluators' efficiency improved as they adapted to a one-pass video inspection approach. Ultimately, the experienced CA was rated more highly for its efficiency, trustworthiness, and comprehensiveness despite initial perceptions of expertise differences.
